title: Define a New Translation Table
text: Define a New Translation Table Define your own device translation table if you want to modify how a device works, or address a device that is not yet supported. Contact the device's distributor for a list of the control characters the device uses. Note: Defining a complete table is difficult and should only be tackled by someone experienced in this area. If you need to define a translation table, it is much easier to duplicate and adapt an existing table than to create a new one from scratch. An existing definition can match your particular device quite closely, if not exactly. Copy and Adapt an Existing Translation Table . For example, you may want to use national character sets or color preferences. If a device does not support a certain feature, such as graphic borderlines or color, the device can find a suitable approximation or leave the feature out. To define a translation table: Click More Editors , then choose Device Tables or Keyboard Tables , according to the type of translation table you are creating. Select or create the library to contain the table. Note: Do not define the table in the USYS default library. It contains the standard definitions delivered with Uniface, and tables defined in this library may be overwritten when a new release of Uniface is installed. Provide a name that corresponds to the output device or keyboard. For example, VT200 for a VT200 terminal, or FX80 for an FX80 printer. In the Definition , edit the contents of the translation table using the appropriate syntax and mnemonics. For more information, see Device Translation Table Syntax and Keyboard Translation Table Syntax . Choose File>Compile to compile the table. Move the compiled definitions to a deployment archive using the Resources Manager. Once a table has been defined, you can import it into other Uniface installations. Related Links Translation Tables To mediate between itself and the external environment, Uniface uses translation tables. Translation tables provide input and output mappings between platform-specific device codes and Uniface functions, and between external and internal character sets. Uniface provides translation tables for the platforms it supports, but you can customize these or use them to create your own tables. Guidelines for Defining Keyboard Translation Tables When defining or adapting keyboard translation tables, there are some general guidelines you should follow. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
